options.markscheme: This corresponds to an error of a factor of 10, typically from using $V=25.0\times10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3$ instead of $2.50\times10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3$, or from a power-of-ten mistake in $n=\frac{PV}{RT}$. The correct value is about $64.4\,\text{g mol}^{-1}$.

Use the ideal gas law: $n=\frac{PV}{RT}$. Convert $P=100\,\text{kPa}=1.00\times10^5\,\text{Pa}$ and $V=25.0\,\text{cm}^3=2.50\times10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3$. Then $n=\frac{(1.00\times10^5)(2.50\times10^{-5})}{8.314\times298}\approx1.01\times10^{-3}\,\text{mol}$. Molar mass $M=\frac{m}{n}=\frac{0.0650\,\text{g}}{1.01\times10^{-3}\,\text{mol}}\approx64.4\,\text{g mol}^{-1}$.

This corresponds to an error of a factor of 10, typically from converting $100\,\text{kPa}$ incorrectly as $1.00\times10^6\,\text{Pa}$ or from a similar factor-of-ten error in $n=\frac{PV}{RT}$. The correct value is about $64.4\,\text{g mol}^{-1}$.
This is the calculated amount of substance $n$ in mol (approximately $1.01\times10^{-3}\,\text{mol}$), not the molar mass. The molar mass is found using $M=\frac{m}{n}\approx64.4\,\text{g mol}^{-1}$.

questions.specification:
A marine biologist studies the effect of ocean acidification on calcium carbonate shells. In the lab, she reacts powdered $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ with excess hydrochloric acid and collects the carbon dioxide gas evolved.
The reaction is:

$$\mathrm{CaCO_3(s)} + 2\mathrm{HCl(aq)} \to \mathrm{CaCl_2(aq)} + \mathrm{H_2O(l)} + \mathrm{CO_2(g)}$$

She starts with a $1.25\ \mathrm{g}$ sample of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ and the gas collected has a volume of $240\ \mathrm{cm^3}$ at room temperature and pressure (RTP, $24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}}$ for gas molar volume).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.1 How much? The amount of chemical change > R2.1.2 Using the mole ratio
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.1.2 Using the mole ratio
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses balanced equation to relate moles of CaCO3 to moles of CO2 produced (mole ratio/stoichiometric relationship), especially in parts (b) and (c).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.1 How much? The amount of chemical change > R2.1.4 Percentage yield
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.1.4 Percentage yield
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) explicitly asks for percentage yield based on actual vs theoretical CO2 volume; part (e) asks why yield is <100%.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.4.1 The mole and Avogadro constant
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) requires converting mass of CaCO3 to moles, and part (d) uses Avogadro’s constant to convert moles of CO2 to number of molecules.

parts.content: Calculate the number of moles of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ in the sample.
parts.markscheme:
Molar mass of $\mathrm{CaCO_3} = 40.1 + 12.0 + (3 \times 16.0) = 100.1\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

$n = \frac{1.25}{100.1} = 0.0125\ \mathrm{mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To find the number of moles of calcium carbonate, we first need to identify the mass of the sample provided in the question. 

This problem falls under **Structure 1.4: Counting particles by mass**, where we learn that the amount of a substance in moles ($n$) is related to its mass ($m$) and molar mass ($M$).

steps.title: Determine the molar mass
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the molar mass ($M$) of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ by adding the relative atomic masses ($A_r$) of all atoms in the formula using the periodic table:

$$\begin{aligned} M(\mathrm{CaCO_3}) &= A_r(\mathrm{Ca}) + A_r(\mathrm{C}) + 3 \times A_r(\mathrm{O}) \\ &= 40.1 + 12.0 + 3(16.0) \\ &= 100.1\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}} \end{aligned}$$

Identifying this value correctly earns you the first mark (**M1**) in the markscheme.

steps.title: Calculate the moles
steps.content:
Now, we use the mass-mole relationship found in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Substitute our values into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{1.25\ \mathrm{g}}{100.1\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}} \\ n &\approx 0.0125\ \mathrm{mol} \end{aligned}$$

Finalizing this calculation with the correct units earns the second mark (**A1**).

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, type 1.25 / 100.1.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles by dividing mass by molar mass.

parts.content: Calculate the theoretical volume of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ that should be produced at RTP.
parts.markscheme: $\text{Volume} = 0.0125\ \mathrm{mol} \times 24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}} = 0.300\ \mathrm{dm^3}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Find moles of calcium carbonate
steps.content:
First, we need to convert the mass of the calcium carbonate ($\mathrm{CaCO_3}$) into moles. We use the formula for moles from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Based on the periodic table, the molar mass ($M$) of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ is approximately:
$$40.08 + 12.01 + 3(16.00) = 100.09\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$$

Using the mass given in the question:
$$n(\mathrm{CaCO_3}) = \frac{1.25\ \mathrm{g}}{100.09\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}} \approx 0.0125\ \mathrm{mol}$$

This calculation is part of **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass**, which is essential for any stoichiometry problem.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.25 divided by 100.09 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of calcium carbonate.

steps.title: Apply the molar ratio
steps.content:
Next, we look at the balanced chemical equation to find the relationship between the reactant and the product. 

$$\mathrm{CaCO_3(s)} + 2\mathrm{HCl(aq)} \to \mathrm{CaCl_2(aq)} + \mathrm{H_2O(l)} + \mathbf{1}\mathrm{CO_2(g)}$$

The molar ratio between $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ and $\mathrm{CO_2}$ is $1:1$. This means that $1$ mole of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ produces $1$ mole of $\mathrm{CO_2}$.

Therefore:
$$n(\mathrm{CO_2}) = n(\mathrm{CaCO_3}) = 0.0125\ \mathrm{mol}$$

Identifying mole ratios is a core skill from **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**.

steps.title: Calculate theoretical gas volume
steps.content:
Finally, we convert the moles of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ gas into a volume at Room Temperature and Pressure (RTP). The question provides the molar volume ($V_m$) as $24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}}$.

The formula for volume is:
$$\text{Volume} = n \times V_m$$

Substituting our values:
$$\text{Volume} = 0.0125\ \mathrm{mol} \times 24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}}$$
$$\text{Volume} = 0.300\ \mathrm{dm^3}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final volume of CO2.

highlights.text: 24.0 dm3 mol-1 for gas molar volume

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The theoretical volume of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ produced is **$0.300\ \mathrm{dm^3}$** (or $300\ \mathrm{cm^3}$).

In the IB markscheme, you earn the mark for correctly multiplying the calculated moles by the molar volume. Ensure you include units in your final answer for clarity!

steps.title: Analyze the stoichiometry
steps.content:
From the balanced chemical equation, we can see the molar ratio between calcium carbonate and carbon dioxide:

$$\mathrm{CaCO_3(s)} + 2\mathrm{HCl(aq)} \to \mathrm{CaCl_2(aq)} + \mathrm{H_2O(l)} + \mathrm{CO_2(g)}$$

The coefficients show that $1$ mole of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ reacts to produce $1$ mole of $\mathrm{CO_2}$. This $1:1$ ratio is essential for setting up our proportion.

steps.title: Establish the molar proportion
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Proportion Method**, we relate the mass of the reactant to the volume of the gas product. 

We know that:
- $1$ mole of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ has a mass of $100.09\text{ g/mol}$.
- $1$ mole of any gas at RTP occupies $24.0\text{ dm}^3$.

Therefore, we can establish the following relationship: 
$$100.09\text{ g of } \mathrm{CaCO_3} \longrightarrow 24.0\text{ dm}^3 \text{ of } \mathrm{CO_2}$$

steps.title: Set up the ratio
steps.content:
Now, we set up a proportion using our actual sample mass of $1.25\text{ g}$ to find the unknown theoretical volume ($V$):

$$\frac{1.25\text{ g}}{V} = \frac{100.09\text{ g}}{24.0\text{ dm}^3}$$

To solve for $V$, we rearrange the equation:

$$V = \frac{1.25 \times 24.0}{100.09}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final volume
steps.content:
Now we perform the calculation. Note that the IB markscheme often rounds the molar mass of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ to $100.09$ or simply $100.0$. Using the specific molar mass provided:

$$V = 0.2997... \text{ dm}^3$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the given data), we get:

$$V = 0.300\text{ dm}^3$$

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation screen, type (1.25 * 24.0) / 100.09 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression (1.25 * 24.0) / 100.09 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the theoretical volume of CO2.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The theoretical volume of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ produced is $0.300\text{ dm}^3$.

This matches the markscheme requirement where $1$ mark is awarded for the correct volume calculation ($0.0125\text{ mol} \times 24.0\text{ dm}^3\text{ mol}^{-1} = 0.300\text{ dm}^3$).

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: The goal is to calculate the theoretical volume of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ gas produced from a $1.25\ \mathrm{g}$ sample of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$. We will use **dimensional analysis** to convert from mass of reactant to volume of product in a single step.

steps.title: Determine conversion factors
steps.content:
To link mass to volume, we need three pieces of information:
1. **Molar Mass ($M$):** For $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$, $M = 40.08 + 12.01 + 3(16.00) = 100.09\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$.
2. **Mole Ratio:** From the balanced equation, $1\ \mathrm{mol}$ of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ produces $1\ \mathrm{mol}$ of $\mathrm{CO_2}$.
3. **Molar Volume ($V_m$):** The question gives this as $24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}}$ at RTP.

This uses the relationship $$n = \frac{m}{M}$$ from the data booklet.

steps.title: Set up dimensional analysis
steps.content:
We arrange our units so that each one cancels out until we are left with the volume of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ in $\mathrm{dm^3}$:

$$\text{Volume } \mathrm{CO_2} = 1.25\ \mathrm{g\ CaCO_3} \times \frac{1\ \mathrm{mol\ CaCO_3}}{100.09\ \mathrm{g\ CaCO_3}} \times \frac{1\ \mathrm{mol\ CO_2}}{1\ \mathrm{mol\ CaCO_3}} \times \frac{24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ CO_2}}{1\ \mathrm{mol\ CO_2}}$$

steps.title: Calculate and round result
steps.content:
Inputting the calculation into a calculator, we find the theoretical volume. Following significant figure rules (3 sig figs based on $1.25\ \mathrm{g}$ and $24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3}$), we get $0.300\ \mathrm{dm^3}$.

This matches the markscheme requirement where $0.0125\ \mathrm{mol}$ of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ is multiplied by the molar volume.

ti84.instructions: Enter the product of the starting mass, the inverse of the molar mass, the stoichiometric ratio, and the molar volume.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the final volume.

calculator.description: Calculate the theoretical volume of carbon dioxide in dm³.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content: The theoretical volume of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ is **$0.300\ \mathrm{dm^3}$**.

parts.content: Determine the percentage yield of the reaction based on the volume of gas collected.
parts.markscheme:
$\text{Yield} = \frac{0.240}{0.300} \times 100$ <marks>M1</marks>

$= 80\%$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate the moles of reactant
steps.content:
First, we need to know how many moles of calcium carbonate ($\mathrm{CaCO_3}$) we started with. We use the formula for moles from the data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{m}{M} \\ n(\mathrm{CaCO_3}) &= \frac{1.25\ \mathrm{g}}{100.09\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}} \\ &\approx 0.0125\ \mathrm{mol} \end{aligned}$$

This concept is covered in **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass: The mole**.

steps.title: Determine theoretical moles of gas
steps.content:
Next, we look at the stoichiometry of the balanced chemical equation. The mole ratio between $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ and $\mathrm{CO_2}$ is $1:1$.

$$\mathrm{CaCO_3(s)} + 2\mathrm{HCl(aq)} \to \mathrm{CaCl_2(aq)} + \mathrm{H_2O(l)} + \mathbf{1}\mathrm{CO_2(g)}$$

Since $1$ mole of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ produces $1$ mole of $\mathrm{CO_2}$, we theoretically expect:

$$\text{Theoretical } n(\mathrm{CO_2}) = 0.0125\ \mathrm{mol}$$

steps.title: Calculate the theoretical volume
steps.content:
Now, we convert those theoretical moles into a volume using the molar volume at RTP ($24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}}$) provided in the question.

$$\begin{aligned} V_{\text{theoretical}} &= n \times V_m \\ V_{\text{theoretical}} &= 0.0125\ \mathrm{mol} \times 24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}} \\ &= 0.300\ \mathrm{dm^3} \end{aligned}$$

Converting this to $\mathrm{cm^3}$ (since the actual volume is in $\mathrm{cm^3}$):
$$0.300\ \mathrm{dm^3} \times 1000 = 300\ \mathrm{cm^3}$$

steps.title: Compare actual and theoretical volumes
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the percentage yield. The percentage yield is the ratio of the actual volume collected to the theoretical volume we just calculated, multiplied by $100$. This is part of **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Percentage Yield} &= \frac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \\ \text{Percentage Yield} &= \frac{240\ \mathrm{cm^3}}{300\ \mathrm{cm^3}} \times 100 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculator Verification
steps.content:
You can use your GDC to quickly perform the final calculation and verify the yield.

$$\text{Percentage Yield} = 80\%$$

**Marking Note:**
*   **M1** is awarded for the correct setup of the yield expression: $\frac{0.240}{0.300} \times 100$ (or using $\mathrm{cm^3}$ units).
*   **A1** is awarded for the final correct answer of $80\%$. Show your units clearly to ensure full marks!

ti84.instructions: Enter the division and multiply by 100.

calculator.description: Calculate percentage yield from actual and theoretical volumes.

steps.title: Identify the approach
steps.content:
To find the percentage yield using the **Mole Comparison** approach, we need to find two values:
1. The **theoretical moles** of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ we expected to produce from the mass of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$.
2. The **actual moles** of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ we actually collected in the lab.

Then, we compare them using the percentage yield formula.

steps.title: Calculate theoretical moles
steps.content:
First, we find the moles of reactant ($\mathrm{CaCO_3}$) used. Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

The molar mass ($M$) of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ is approximately $40.08 + 12.01 + 3(16.00) = 100.09\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$.

$$\text{Theoretical } n(\mathrm{CaCO_3}) = \frac{1.25\ \mathrm{g}}{100.09\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}} \approx 0.0125\ \mathrm{mol}$$

According to the balanced equation, $1\ \text{mole}$ of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ produces $1\ \text{mole}$ of $\mathrm{CO_2}$. Therefore, the theoretical yield is **$0.0125\ \mathrm{mol}$** of $\mathrm{CO_2}$.

calculator.description: Calculate the theoretical moles of calcium carbonate.

steps.title: Calculate actual moles
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the actual moles of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ collected. We must first convert the volume from $\mathrm{cm^3}$ to $\mathrm{dm^3}$ because the molar volume is given in $\mathrm{dm^3}$.

$$V = \frac{240\ \mathrm{cm^3}}{1000} = 0.240\ \mathrm{dm^3}$$

Using the gas molar volume ($V_m = 24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}}$):

$$\text{Actual } n(\mathrm{CO_2}) = \frac{V}{V_m} = \frac{0.240\ \mathrm{dm^3}}{24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}}} = 0.0100\ \mathrm{mol}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the actual moles of carbon dioxide gas.

steps.title: Calculate percentage yield
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the percentage yield by comparing the actual moles to the theoretical moles:

$$\text{Percentage Yield} = \frac{\text{Actual Moles}}{\text{Theoretical Moles}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Percentage Yield} = \frac{0.0100\ \mathrm{mol}}{0.0125\ \mathrm{mol}} \times 100 = 80\%$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final percentage yield.

steps.title: Convert volume and calculate moles
steps.content:
First, we need to determine the actual number of moles of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ gas produced. We use the molar volume of a gas ($V_m$) at room temperature and pressure (RTP). Note that the volume must be in $\mathrm{dm^3}$ for the units to match.

$$\begin{aligned} V_{\mathrm{CO_2}} &= 240\ \mathrm{cm^3} = 0.240\ \mathrm{dm^3} \\ n_{\mathrm{CO_2}} &= \frac{V}{V_m} \\ n_{\mathrm{CO_2}} &= \frac{0.240\ \mathrm{dm^3}}{24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}}} \\ n_{\mathrm{CO_2}} &= 0.0100\ \mathrm{mol} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine required moles of reactant
steps.content:
Using the balanced chemical equation, we find the mole ratio between the reactant $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ and the product $\mathrm{CO_2}$:

$$\mathrm{CaCO_3(s)} + 2\mathrm{HCl(aq)} \to \mathrm{CaCl_2(aq)} + \mathrm{H_2O(l)} + \mathrm{CO_2(g)}$$

The ratio is $1:1$. Therefore, to produce $0.0100\ \text{mol}$ of $\mathrm{CO_2}$, the reaction required exactly $0.0100\ \text{mol}$ of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$.

steps.title: Calculate required mass of CaCO3
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the mass of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ that corresponds to $0.0100\ \text{mol}$. First, find the molar mass ($M$) of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$:

$$M = 40.08 + 12.01 + 3(16.00) = 100.09\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$$

Now, calculate the mass ($m$) using the formula from the data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= n \times M \\ m &= 0.0100\ \mathrm{mol} \times 100.09\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}} \\ m &= 1.0009\ \mathrm{g} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the calculated moles by the molar mass.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the moles of CO2 by the molar mass of CaCO3.

calculator.description: Calculate the theoretical mass of calcium carbonate required for the actual gas volume.

steps.title: Calculate the percentage yield
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the percentage yield by comparing the mass of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ that actually reacted ($1.0009\ \text{g}$) to the initial mass of the sample ($1.25\ \text{g}$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Percentage Yield} &= \frac{\text{Theoretical mass required}}{\text{Actual starting mass}} \times 100 \\ \text{Percentage Yield} &= \frac{1.0009\ \mathrm{g}}{1.25\ \mathrm{g}} \times 100 \\ \text{Percentage Yield} &\approx 80.1\% \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to significant figures as shown in the markscheme, we get **$80\%$**.

gdc.instructions: Divide required mass by initial mass and multiply by 100.

ti84.instructions: Divide the calculated required mass by the initial mass and multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Divide the mass required by the actual mass and multiply by 100.

calculator.description: Calculate the final percentage yield.

parts.content: Calculate the number of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ molecules collected using Avogadro’s constant.
parts.markscheme:
$n = \frac{0.240}{24.0} = 0.0100\ \mathrm{mol}$ <marks>M1</marks>

$\text{Molecules} = 0.0100 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} = 6.02 \times 10^{21}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Convert volume to dm³
steps.content:
To work with the molar gas volume constant provided, we first need to ensure our units match. We convert the volume of gas from $\mathrm{cm^3}$ to $\mathrm{dm^3}$ by dividing by $1000$.

$$V = \frac{240\ \mathrm{cm^3}}{1000} = 0.240\ \mathrm{dm^3}$$

calculator.description: Convert cm³ to dm³

steps.title: Calculate moles of CO2
steps.content:
Using the concept of molar volume (covered in **S1.5.3 Molar volume of an ideal gas**), we calculate the number of moles ($n$) of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ by dividing the volume of gas by the molar volume at RTP ($V_{\text{m}}$).

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{V}{V_{\text{m}}} \\ n &= \frac{0.240\ \mathrm{dm^3}}{24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3\ mol^{-1}}} \\ n &= 0.0100\ \mathrm{mol} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns the first mark (**M1**).

gdc.instructions: Divide the volume in dm³ by 24.0

calculator.description: Calculate moles of CO2

steps.title: Find the total molecules
steps.content:
Now, we convert moles to the number of molecules using Avogadro’s constant ($N_{\text{A}}$), as seen in **S1.4.1 The mole and Avogadro constant**. 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Molecules} &= n \times N_{\text{A}} \\ \text{Molecules} &= 0.0100\ \text{mol} \times (6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}) \\ \text{Molecules} &= 6.02 \times 10^{21} \end{aligned}$$

This final value earns the second mark (**A1**).

gdc.instructions: Multiply the moles by Avogadro's constant

calculator.description: Calculate total molecules

steps.title: Identify conversion factors
steps.content:
To find the number of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ molecules, we need to convert the given volume ($240\ \mathrm{cm^3}$) through several steps: 
1. **Volume conversion**: $\mathrm{cm^3}$ to $\mathrm{dm^3}$ (using $1\ \mathrm{dm^3} = 1000\ \mathrm{cm^3}$).
2. **Molar volume**: $\mathrm{dm^3}$ to $\mathrm{mol}$ (using the provided RTP molar volume).
3. **Avogadro's constant**: $\mathrm{mol}$ to molecules.

This calculation uses concepts from **Topic S1.4: The mole and Avogadro constant**.

steps.title: Set up dimensional analysis
steps.content:
Using the dimensional analysis approach, we arrange these factors so that the units cancel out, leaving us with the number of molecules:

$$\text{Molecules} = 240\ \mathrm{cm^3} \times \frac{1\ \mathrm{dm^3}}{1000\ \mathrm{cm^3}} \times \frac{1\ \mathrm{mol}}{24.0\ \mathrm{dm^3}} \times \frac{6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{molecules}}{1\ \mathrm{mol}}$$

Notice how $\mathrm{cm^3}$, $\mathrm{dm^3}$, and $\mathrm{mol}$ all cancel out!

steps.title: Calculate the result
steps.content:
Now we perform the calculation in one sequence. First, the volume in $\mathrm{dm^3}$ is $0.240$, then the amount in moles is $0.0100\ \mathrm{mol}$, and finally we multiply by Avogadro's constant:

$$\text{Molecules} = \frac{240}{1000 \times 24.0} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}$$

$$\text{Molecules} = 6.02 \times 10^{21}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter (240 / 1000) / 24.0 * 6.02E23 into the calculator and evaluate.

ti84.instructions: Enter (240 / 1000) / 24.0 * 6.02 * 10^23 into the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of molecules in one step.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The number of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ molecules collected is $6.02 \times 10^{21}$.

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark (M1)** for correctly determining the moles ($0.0100\ \mathrm{mol}$) and **1 mark (A1)** for the final correct number of molecules.

steps.title: Unify volume units
steps.content:
To compare the collected gas to the molar volume, we first need to ensure the units match. The gas was collected in $\text{cm}^3$, but the molar volume is given in $\text{dm}^3$. 

Since $1000\text{ cm}^3 = 1\text{ dm}^3$:

$$\text{Volume in dm}^3 = \frac{240\text{ cm}^3}{1000} = 0.240\text{ dm}^3$$

calculator.description: Convert cm³ to dm³

steps.title: Determine the mole fraction
steps.content:
Using proportional reasoning, we determine what fraction of a full mole ($24.0\text{ dm}^3$) our sample represents. This gives us the number of moles ($n$) collected:

$$n = \frac{\text{Volume collected}}{\text{Molar volume}} = \frac{0.240\text{ dm}^3}{24.0\text{ dm}^3\text{ mol}^{-1}}$$

$$n = 0.0100\text{ mol}$$

This calculation earns the first mark **(M1)** for correctly finding the amount in moles.

calculator.description: Calculate moles of gas

steps.title: Calculate number of molecules
steps.content:
One mole of any substance contains Avogadro's constant ($N_A$) of particles. Since we have $0.0100$ moles, we multiply this fraction by $N_A$ to find the total number of molecules:

$$\text{Molecules} = n \times N_A$$

$$\text{Molecules} = 0.0100 \times (6.02 \times 10^{23})$$

$$\text{Molecules} = 6.02 \times 10^{21}$$

This final value earns the second mark **(A1)**.

calculator.description: Calculate total molecules

parts.content: State one possible reason why the percentage yield is less than 100%.
parts.markscheme: Some gas escaped before being collected / reaction was incomplete <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the gas product
steps.content:
In this experiment, the reaction between calcium carbonate and hydrochloric acid produces carbon dioxide as a gas, which is being collected to measure the yield.

$$\mathrm{CaCO_3(s) + 2HCl(aq) \to CaCl_2(aq) + H_2O(l) + CO_2(g)}$$

steps.title: Analyze the experimental procedure
steps.content:
When performing this reaction in a lab, the researcher must add the **powdered $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$** to the flask containing the acid and then quickly seal it with a stopper connected to a gas syringe. 

Because the reaction starts the moment the reactants touch, there is a small time interval before the system is sealed. During this delay, some of the produced $\mathrm{CO_2}$ gas escapes into the room instead of being captured in the collection apparatus.

steps.title: Consider apparatus limitations
steps.content: Another practical factor is that the apparatus may not be 100% airtight. Small leaks in the joints of the glassware or the gas syringe itself can allow gas to escape the system throughout the duration of the reaction.

steps.title: State the final reason
steps.content:
Based on the **Practical Gas Loss** approach, the most likely reason for a yield lower than 100% is that some of the gas escaped from the system before it could be measured.

**Reason:** Some gas escaped before being collected.

steps.title: Reviewing stoichiometry
steps.content: This question relates to **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**. When we calculate a theoretical yield, we assume that the reaction goes perfectly and every particle of reactant is converted into product.

steps.title: Defining the yield gap
steps.content: In this experiment, the scientist found that the **percentage yield is less than 100%**. This means the $240\ \mathrm{cm^3}$ of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ collected is less than the volume we would expect to get from the $1.25\ \mathrm{g}$ sample of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ if the reaction was perfect.

steps.title: Reason: Incomplete reaction
steps.content: One possible reason for this is that the **reaction was incomplete**. In a lab setting, a reaction might stop before all of the solid $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ has reacted with the acid. If some of the reactant remains at the bottom of the flask, fewer moles of gas are produced, leading to a lower experimental yield.

steps.title: Recall percentage yield
steps.content:
In chemistry, the **percentage yield** tells us how efficient a reaction was. It compares the amount of product we actually collected in the lab (the actual yield) to the maximum amount we could have theoretically produced (the theoretical yield).

If the yield is less than $100\%$, it means some factor caused the actual amount of $\text{CO}_2$ collected to be lower than what our calculations predicted. You can find more about this in **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**.

steps.title: Analyze the reactant purity
steps.content: When we calculate the theoretical yield, we use the mass of the starting material. In this experiment, we used a $1.25\ \text{g}$ sample of $\text{CaCO}_3$. Our calculations assume that every single milligram of that $1.25\ \text{g}$ is pure calcium carbonate.

steps.title: Explain the lower yield
steps.content:
One common reason for a yield below $100\%$ is that the **initial reactant sample was impure**. 

If the sample contained non-reactive impurities (like sand or other minerals), the actual mass of $\text{CaCO}_3$ available to react would be less than $1.25\ \text{g}$. Because there is less actual reactant than we used in our calculation, less $\text{CO}_2$ gas is produced, resulting in a lower percentage yield.

steps.title: Understand percentage yield
steps.content:
In chemistry, the **percentage yield** compares the amount of product actually collected to the maximum amount that could theoretically be produced. This is covered in **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**. 

$$\text{Percentage Yield} = \frac{\text{Actual Yield}}{\text{Theoretical Yield}} \times 100\%$$ 

If the yield is less than $100\%$, it means some of our product (the $CO_2$ gas) was not accounted for in the final measurement.

steps.title: Analyze the gas collection
steps.content:
In this experiment, $CO_2$ is produced in an aqueous environment (the $HCl$ solution) and often collected over water. A key characteristic of carbon dioxide is its **solubility**. 

Unlike some gases that are very insoluble, carbon dioxide can dissolve in the water present in the reaction flask or the water used in the collection apparatus. If $CO_2$ molecules stay in the liquid phase, they are not "evolved" as gas to be measured.

highlights.text: collects the carbon dioxide gas evolved

steps.title: Identify the specific reason
steps.content:
Because some of the product dissolves into the aqueous hydrochloric acid or the collection water, the volume of gas measured ($240\ \text{cm}^3$) is lower than the theoretical volume. 

Therefore, a valid reason for a lower yield is that **some carbon dioxide dissolved in the solution**.

parts.content: Suggest one method the student could use to improve the accuracy of the experiment.
parts.markscheme: Use a gas syringe for more accurate measurement of gas volume <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the experimental setup
steps.content: To improve accuracy, we first need to consider how the gas is being collected. In many introductory experiments, gas is collected by displacing water in a measuring cylinder. However, $\mathrm{CO_2}$ presents a specific challenge because it is slightly soluble in water, especially when it reacts to form carbonic acid.

highlights.text: Suggest one method the student could use to improve the accuracy of the experiment.

steps.title: Switch to a gas syringe
steps.content:
The most effective way to improve the accuracy of the volume measurement is to **use a gas syringe**. 

A gas syringe is connected directly to the reaction flask via a delivery tube. As the reaction proceeds, the gas enters the syringe and pushes the plunger out, allowing you to read the volume directly from the scale on the syringe barrel.

highlights.text: Use a gas syringe for more accurate measurement of gas volume

steps.title: Justify the improvement
steps.content:
Using a gas syringe improves accuracy for two main reasons:

1.  **Prevents Dissolving**: Unlike the water displacement method, the gas does not come into contact with water, so no $\mathrm{CO_2}$ is lost due to dissolution.
2.  **Higher Precision**: Gas syringes often have finer graduations than large measuring cylinders, which reduces the random error in reading the final volume.

This method aligns with the experimental techniques discussed in **Topic 1.5: Ideal Gases** regarding the measurement of molar volumes.

steps.title: Identify the source of error
steps.content:
In this experiment, the reaction begins the moment the hydrochloric acid touches the **powdered $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$**. Because the powder has a high surface area, the reaction is very fast. If the student adds the acid to the flask and then tries to insert a stopper, some of the carbon dioxide gas will escape into the room before the system is sealed. 

This creates a systematic error where the measured volume of gas is always lower than the actual volume produced. By calculating the theoretical yield, we can see a significant gap:

ti84.instructions:
1. Find moles of CaCO3: 1.25 / 100.09
2. Multiply by molar volume: Ans * 24000

calculator.description: Calculate the expected volume of gas (Theoretical Yield) to see the extent of the loss.

steps.title: Use a divided flask
steps.content:
To prevent this initial loss of gas, the student can use a **divided flask** (sometimes called a 'W' flask) or place one of the reactants in a small vial (ignition tube) suspended inside the main flask. 

This method allows the reactants to be kept separate while the apparatus is being set up and sealed.

steps.title: Seal before mixing
steps.content:
The improvement involves a simple change in procedure:
1. Place the $\mathrm{HCl}$ in one side of the divided flask and the $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ in the other (or place the solid in a vial inside the acid flask).
2. Insert the stopper and connect the gas collection apparatus so the system is completely airtight.
3. **Tilt the flask** to allow the reactants to mix *after* the seal is secure.

This ensures that every bubble of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ produced is captured from the very start of the reaction.

steps.title: Evaluate the improvement
steps.content:
While the markscheme identifies that a gas syringe provides a more precise measurement of volume, the **Prevent Initial Gas Escape** approach addresses a major procedural error. 

By ensuring no gas is lost during the setup, the student moves the measured volume ($240\ \mathrm{cm^3}$) much closer to the theoretical value (approx. $300\ \mathrm{cm^3}$), significantly improving the **accuracy** of the experiment.

highlights.text: gas collected has a volume of $240\ \mathrm{cm^3}$
Use a gas syringe for more accurate measurement of gas volume

steps.title: Understand percentage uncertainty
steps.content:
In any experiment, every measurement has an inherent absolute uncertainty (error). To improve accuracy, we want to minimize the **percentage uncertainty**, which is calculated as:

$$\text{Percentage Uncertainty} = \frac{\text{Absolute Uncertainty}}{\text{Measured Value}} \times 100\%$$

By increasing the denominator (the measured value), we can make the overall percentage uncertainty smaller without changing the equipment.

steps.title: Increase reactant mass
steps.content:
One effective way to improve accuracy is to **increase the mass of the calcium carbonate ($\mathrm{CaCO_3}$)**. 

Currently, the student uses a $1.25\mathrm{g}$ sample. If we increase this mass (while keeping the hydrochloric acid in excess), the stoichiometry of the reaction dictates that a larger amount of carbon dioxide gas will be produced.

steps.title: Analyze the volume effect
steps.content:
The gas collected has a volume of $240\mathrm{cm^3}$. If we double the mass of $\mathrm{CaCO_3}$ to $2.50\mathrm{g}$, the volume of gas produced would double to $480\mathrm{cm^3}$. 

Since the absolute uncertainty of the measuring device (like a gas syringe or graduated cylinder) remains the same, measuring a larger volume significantly reduces the percentage error associated with that reading.

gdc.instructions: Divide the absolute error by the measured volumes and multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the percentage uncertainty for both volumes.

calculator.description: Compare percentage uncertainty for a constant absolute error of 1 cm³.

steps.title: State final improvement
steps.content: Therefore, a valid method to improve accuracy is to **increase the mass of the calcium carbonate used**. This generates a larger volume of gas, thereby reducing the percentage uncertainty in the gas volume measurement.

parts.content: Define the term mole.
parts.markscheme:
- State the definition: The amount of substance that contains $6.02 \times 10^{23}$ particles (atoms, molecules, etc.) <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Understand the concept
steps.content: In chemistry, because atoms and molecules are so incredibly small, we need a way to group them into manageable numbers. This is where the concept of the **mole** comes in. It is used to measure the **amount of substance**, much like a "dozen" describes 12 items. This concept is covered in **Structure 1.4: Counting particles by mass: The mole**.

steps.title: Identify Avogadro's constant
steps.content:
To define the mole using the particle-based approach, we look at the specific number of elementary particles required to make up exactly one mole. This value is known as the **Avogadro constant** ($N_A$), which you can find in the **Physical Constants** section of your data booklet.

$$N_{\text{A}} = 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: State the definition
steps.content:
By combining these ideas, we define the mole as follows: 

A **mole** is the amount of substance that contains exactly $$6.02 \times 10^{23}$$ elementary particles. 

These "particles" can be atoms, molecules, ions, or electrons depending on the substance being measured.

steps.title: Check markscheme requirements
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must mention both the **amount of substance** and the specific value of the **Avogadro constant**. 

**Final Answer:** 
The amount of substance that contains $6.02 \times 10^{23}$ particles.

steps.title: Understanding the Mole Concept
steps.content: In chemistry, we need a way to count an enormous number of tiny particles (like atoms or molecules). To do this, we use the **mole**, which is the SI unit for the "amount of substance". This fundamental concept is explored in **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass: The mole**.

steps.title: The Carbon-12 Standard
steps.content:
The **Carbon-12 Reference** approach defines the mole based on a specific physical standard. 

A mole is defined as the amount of substance that contains the same number of elementary particles (atoms, molecules, ions, etc.) as there are atoms in exactly $12\text{ g}$ of the isotope Carbon-12 ($^{12}\text{C}$).

steps.title: Identifying the Avogadro Constant
steps.content:
The number of atoms found in $12\text{ g}$ of Carbon-12 is a constant known as the **Avogadro constant** ($N_{\text{A}}$). You can find this value in the **Physical Constants** section of your data booklet:

$$N_{\text{A}} = 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Stating the Final Definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you should state that the mole is the amount of substance containing $6.02 \times 10^{23}$ particles. This matches the number of atoms in $12\text{ g}$ of Carbon-12.

**Definition:** The amount of substance that contains $6.02 \times 10^{23}$ elementary particles.

parts.content: Calculate the number of moles in $18.0\ \mathrm{g}$ of water.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the amount of substance: $n = \frac{18.0\ \mathrm{g}}{18.0\ \mathrm{g\ mol}^{-1}} = 1.00\ \mathrm{mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

parts.content: State the number of water molecules in this sample.
parts.markscheme:
- State the number of molecules: $1.00 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} = 6.02 \times 10^{23}$ molecules <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To solve this, we first need to know how many moles we are working with. From the previous context, we have a sample of water containing $1.00 \text{ mol}$. 

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass: The mole**. If you're new to this, remember that a mole is just a specific number of particles, similar to how a 'dozen' means 12.

steps.title: Find the Avogadro constant
steps.content:
To convert from moles to the actual number of molecules, we use the **Avogadro constant** ($N_A$). You can find this value in the **Physical Constants** section of your IB Data Booklet.

$$N_A = 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate total molecules
steps.content:
The number of particles ($N$) is found by multiplying the amount in moles ($n$) by the Avogadro constant ($N_A$):

$$\begin{aligned} N &= n \times N_A \\ N &= 1.00 \text{ mol} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1} \\ N &= 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 1.00 * 6.02, then use the scientific notation button (EXP or E) and enter 23.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.00, then multiply by 6.02, then use the [EE] key (2nd + comma) followed by 23.

desmos.instructions: Type 1.00 * 6.02 * 10^23 to see the result in scientific notation.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of molecules using scientific notation.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content: The sample contains $6.02 \times 10^{23}$ water molecules. Since the initial value ($1.00$) has three significant figures, we provide the final answer to three significant figures as well.

parts.content: State the chemical formula of calcium carbonate.
parts.markscheme:
- State the formula: $\text{CaCO}_3$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the compound
steps.content: The question asks for the chemical formula of **calcium carbonate**, which is a common compound studied in the context of stoichiometry and acid-base reactions.

steps.title: Recall the formula
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, calcium carbonate is frequently encountered as an antacid or a reactant in gas-forming reactions. By recalling the symbols for the calcium ion ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$) and the polyatomic carbonate ion ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$), we can state the formula directly:

$$\text{CaCO}_3$$

steps.title: Identify the ions
steps.content: To determine the chemical formula, we first identify the two parts of the compound: the metal cation (calcium) and the polyatomic anion (carbonate). According to the **Ionic Model (Structure 2.1)**, these ions combine in a ratio that results in zero overall charge.

steps.title: Determine calcium's charge
steps.content:
Calcium ($Ca$) is located in **Group 2** of the periodic table. As a metal in Group 2, it loses its two valence electrons to form a stable cation with a $2+$ charge.

$$\text{Calcium ion} = \text{Ca}^{2+}$$

steps.title: Identify the carbonate ion
steps.content:
The carbonate ion is a common polyatomic ion you should recognize from your studies of chemical formulas. It consists of one carbon and three oxygen atoms and carries a $2-$ charge.

$$\text{Carbonate ion} = \text{CO}_3^{2-}$$

steps.title: Balance the charges
steps.content:
To form a neutral compound, the total positive charge must equal the total negative charge. 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total positive charge} &= +2 \\ \text{Total negative charge} &= -2 \\ (+2) + (-2) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Since the charges are already equal and opposite, the ions combine in a **1:1 ratio**.

steps.title: State the final formula
steps.content:
By combining one calcium ion with one carbonate ion, we get the final chemical formula.

$$\text{CaCO}_3$$

parts.content: Calculate the molar mass of calcium carbonate.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the molar mass: $40.1 + 12.0 + 3(16.0) = 100.1\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the chemical formula
steps.content:
To calculate the molar mass, we first need to identify the chemical formula for calcium carbonate. Based on the chemical name and standard nomenclature, the formula is:

$$\text{CaCO}_3$$

This tells us that one formula unit contains one calcium atom, one carbon atom, and three oxygen atoms. If you're reviewing the basics of formulas, this is covered in **Topic S1.1.1: Elements, Compounds and Mixtures**.

steps.title: Find relative atomic masses
steps.content:
Next, we look up the relative atomic masses ($A_r$) for each element in the Periodic Table of the IB Data Booklet. For most IB chemistry problems, we use values rounded to at least one or two decimal places:

- Calcium ($Ca$): $40.08 \approx 40.1$
- Carbon ($C$): $12.01 \approx 12.0$
- Oxygen ($O$): $16.00$

steps.title: Sum the atomic masses
steps.content:
Using the "Summation of Atomic Masses" approach, we add the masses of all atoms present in the formula. Remember to multiply the mass of oxygen by 3 because of the subscript in $\text{CaCO}_3$.

$$\begin{aligned} M(\text{CaCO}_3) &= A_r(\text{Ca}) + A_r(\text{C}) + 3 \times A_r(\text{O}) \\ M(\text{CaCO}_3) &= 40.1 + 12.0 + 3(16.0) \end{aligned}$$

This concept is found in **Topic S1.4.3: Molar mass**.

gdc.instructions: Enter the addition and multiplication into the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Type the expression into the main screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the total molar mass of calcium carbonate.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
After performing the calculation, we find the molar mass. Ensure you include the correct units, which are $\text{g mol}^{-1}$ for molar mass.

$$M = 100.1\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$$

parts.content: Calculate the amount, in moles, of calcium carbonate in the tablet.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the amount in moles: $n = \frac{5.00\ \text{g}}{100.1\ \text{g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0500\ \text{mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Calculate the molar mass
steps.content:
To find the number of moles, we first need the molar mass ($M$) of calcium carbonate, $\text{CaCO}_3$. We calculate this by summing the relative atomic masses ($A_r$) of all atoms in the formula from the periodic table:

$$\begin{aligned} M(\text{CaCO}_3) &= A_r(\text{Ca}) + A_r(\text{C}) + 3 \times A_r(\text{O}) \\ &= 40.08 + 12.01 + 3(16.00) \\ &= 100.09 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

For simplicity in calculations, we can use the value **100.1 g mol⁻¹** as shown in the markscheme.

gdc.instructions: Enter the sum of the atomic masses.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the atomic masses.

desmos.instructions: Enter the sum of the atomic masses.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of calcium carbonate.

steps.title: Identify the mole formula
steps.content:
Next, we use the relationship between mass ($m$), molar mass ($M$), and amount in moles ($n$) found in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute the given mass of the tablet and the calculated molar mass into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{5.00 \text{ g}}{100.1 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ n &\approx 0.0500 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

Note that we provide the answer to **three significant figures** to match the precision of the mass given in the question ($5.00 \text{ g}$).

gdc.instructions: Divide the mass by the molar mass.

ti84.instructions: Divide the mass by the molar mass.

desmos.instructions: Divide the mass by the molar mass.

calculator.description: Calculate the amount in moles.

parts.content: Calculate the number of calcium ions present in this amount.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the number of ions: $0.0500 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} = 3.01 \times 10^{22}\ \text{calcium ions}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Calculate the molar mass
steps.content:
To find the number of moles, we first need the molar mass ($M$) of calcium carbonate ($CaCO_3$). Using the relative atomic masses from the periodic table:

- $Ca = 40.08\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$
- $C = 12.01\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$
- $O = 16.00\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$

$$M(CaCO_3) = 40.08 + 12.01 + (3 \times 16.00) = 100.09\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$$

For simplicity in many IB problems, this is often rounded to $100.09\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$ or just $100.1\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Calculate the amount in moles
steps.content:
Now we use the formula from the data booklet to find the number of moles ($n$) in the given mass ($m$).

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Substituting the values from the question:

$$n = \frac{5.00\ \text{g}}{100.09\ \text{g mol}^{-1}} = 0.049955...\ \text{mol}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $0.0500\ \text{mol}$.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles by dividing mass by molar mass.

steps.title: Find the number of ions
steps.content:
One formula unit of $CaCO_3$ contains exactly one $Ca^{2+}$ ion. Therefore, the number of moles of calcium ions is equal to the number of moles of $CaCO_3$.

To find the actual number of particles, we multiply the number of moles by the Avogadro constant ($N_A$):

$$\text{Number of ions} = n \times N_A$$
$$\text{Number of ions} = 0.0500\ \text{mol} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$$
$$\text{Number of ions} = 3.01 \times 10^{22}$$

gdc.instructions: Input 0.0500 multiplied by 6.02 times 10 to the power of 23.

calculator.description: Multiply the number of moles by Avogadro's constant to find the total number of ions.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The number of calcium ions present in the $5.00\ \text{g}$ tablet is $3.01 \times 10^{22}$.

This calculation directly matches the markscheme requirement for 1 mark.

steps.title: Identify the conversion path
steps.content:
To solve this using **Dimensional Analysis**, we need to link the mass of the tablet to the total number of calcium ions. We will follow this conversion path:

$$\text{Mass of } \text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{moles of } \text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{moles of } \text{Ca}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{number of } \text{Ca}^{2+} \text{ ions}$$

This involves using the molar mass of $\text{CaCO}_3$ and the Avogadro constant.

steps.title: Calculate the molar mass
steps.content:
First, we determine the molar mass ($M$) of calcium carbonate ($\text{CaCO}_3$) to use as our first conversion factor. Using the periodic table:

- $\text{Ca} = 40.08\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$
- $\text{C} = 12.01\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$
- $\text{O} = 16.00\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$

$$M(\text{CaCO}_3) = 40.08 + 12.01 + (3 \times 16.00) = 100.09\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Set up dimensional analysis
steps.content:
Now, we set up a single expression where units cancel out to leave us with "ions of $\text{Ca}^{2+}$". 

Note that in one formula unit of $\text{CaCO}_3$, there is exactly **one** $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ ion. We will also use the Avogadro constant from the data booklet:

$$\text{Number of ions} = 5.00\ \text{g CaCO}_3 \times \frac{1\ \text{mol CaCO}_3}{100.09\ \text{g CaCO}_3} \times \frac{1\ \text{mol Ca}^{2+}}{1\ \text{mol CaCO}_3} \times \frac{6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{ions}}{1\ \text{mol Ca}^{2+}}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final answer
steps.content:
Multiplying across the top and dividing by the bottom, we get:

$$\text{Number of ions} = \frac{5.00 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}}{100.09} = 3.01 \times 10^{22}\ \text{calcium ions}$$

This result is rounded to 3 significant figures to match the given mass ($5.00\ \text{g}$).

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, type (5 / 100.09) * 6.02 * 10^23 and press EXE.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of ions using the dimensional analysis setup.

questions.specification:
A student investigates the decomposition of hydrated copper(II) sulfate by heating a blue crystalline sample in a crucible.

$$\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s}) \to \mathrm{CuSO}_4(\mathrm{s}) + 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$$

The initial mass of hydrated copper(II) sulfate was $6.25\text{ g}$ and after heating, the mass of the remaining anhydrous solid was $3.98\text{ g}$.

parts.content: Calculate the molar mass of $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate the molar mass:
$63.5 + 32.1 + (4 \times 16.0) + 5 \times (2 \times 1.0 + 16.0) = 249.6\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify components of the formula
steps.content:
To calculate the molar mass ($M$) of $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$, we first identify the number of atoms for each element in the formula:

*   **Cu**: 1 atom
*   **S**: 1 atom
*   **O**: 4 atoms (in $\text{SO}_4$) + 5 atoms (in $5\text{H}_2\text{O}$) = 9 atoms total
*   **H**: $5 \times 2 = 10$ atoms

This calculation belongs to **Topic 1.4: The mole**, which introduces the concept of relative formula masses.

steps.title: Find relative atomic masses
steps.content:
Using the IB Periodic Table, we find the relative atomic mass ($A_r$) for each element. Based on the markscheme, we use the following rounded values:

*   $A_r(\text{Cu}) = 63.5$
*   $A_r(\text{S}) = 32.1$
*   $A_r(\text{O}) = 16.0$
*   $A_r(\text{H}) = 1.0$

steps.title: Sum the atomic masses
steps.content:
We calculate the molar mass by summing the masses of all atoms. It is often easier to calculate the anhydrous salt ($\text{CuSO}_4$) and the water molecules ($5\text{H}_2\text{O}$) separately and then add them together:

$$\begin{aligned} M(\text{CuSO}_4) &= 63.5 + 32.1 + (4 \times 16.0) = 159.6 \\ M(5\text{H}_2\text{O}) &= 5 \times [(2 \times 1.0) + 16.0] = 5 \times 18.0 = 90.0 \end{aligned}$$

Adding these together gives the total molar mass:

$$M = 159.6 + 90.0 = 249.6 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: On the main calculation screen, input the expression for the sum of atomic masses.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the atomic masses directly on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: In a new expression line, type the calculation for the molar mass.

calculator.description: Calculate the total molar mass by summing all atomic contributions.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The molar mass of hydrated copper(II) sulfate is **$249.6 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$**.

In an IB exam, showing the full summation ensures you receive the mark even if you make a minor calculation error. Always include the correct units: $\text{g mol}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Identify the components
steps.content:
To find the molar mass of the hydrated salt $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$, we will use the **Component-Based Summation** approach. This means we treat the formula as a combination of two distinct parts: one unit of anhydrous copper(II) sulfate ($\text{CuSO}_4$) and five units of water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$).

This concept is part of **S1.4.3 Molar mass** in your syllabus.

highlights.text: Calculate the molar mass of CuSO4 . 5H2O.

steps.title: Mass of anhydrous salt
steps.content:
First, we calculate the molar mass of the anhydrous component, $\text{CuSO}_4$. Using the relative atomic masses ($A_r$) provided in the IB data booklet (rounded to one decimal place as per the markscheme):

- $\text{Cu} = 63.5$
- $\text{S} = 32.1$
- $\text{O} = 16.0$

$$\text{Molar Mass of CuSO}_4 = 63.5 + 32.1 + (4 \times 16.0) = 159.6 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Type the expression into the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the atomic masses into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression into the calculator bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of anhydrous copper(II) sulfate.

steps.title: Mass of water component
steps.content:
Next, we find the molar mass of a single water molecule ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$):

- $\text{H} = 1.0$
- $\text{O} = 16.0$

$$\text{Molar Mass of H}_2\text{O} = (2 \times 1.0) + 16.0 = 18.0 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the sum of the components of water.

ti84.instructions: Sum the masses for 2 Hydrogen atoms and 1 Oxygen atom.

desmos.instructions: Sum the atomic masses of H and O.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of one water molecule.

steps.title: Sum the components
steps.content:
Finally, we sum the anhydrous part and the five units of water to get the total molar mass of the hydrated salt:

$$\text{Total Molar Mass} = 159.6 + 5 \times (18.0)$$

Substituting the full expression as shown in the markscheme:

$$63.5 + 32.1 + (4 \times 16.0) + 5 \times (2 \times 1.0 + 16.0) = 249.6 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the final addition of the two components.

ti84.instructions: Add the mass of the anhydrous salt to 5 times the mass of water.

calculator.description: Calculate the final sum for the hydrated salt.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The molar mass of $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is **$249.6 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$**.

steps.title: Calculate mass of water lost
steps.content:
To find how much water was removed during heating, we subtract the mass of the anhydrous solid from the initial mass of the hydrated sample.

$$m_{\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}} = 6.25\text{ g} - 3.98\text{ g} = 2.27\text{ g}$$

steps.title: Convert water mass to moles
steps.content:
Using the relationship between mass ($m$), molar mass ($M$), and moles ($n$), we calculate the amount of water lost. The molar mass of water ($18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$) is derived from $2 \times 1.01 + 16.00$.

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{m}{M} \\ n_{\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}} &= \frac{2.27\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ n_{\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}} &\approx 0.126\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the mass of water by its molar mass.

ti84.instructions: Divide the mass of water by its molar mass.

desmos.instructions: Divide the mass of water by its molar mass.

calculator.description: Calculate moles of water from mass.

steps.title: Find moles of hydrate
steps.content:
From the formula $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot \mathbf{5}\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$, we see the mole ratio of hydrate to water is $1:5$. Thus, the moles of the hydrate is one-fifth of the moles of water.

$$n_{\text{hydrate}} = \frac{0.126\text{ mol}}{5} \approx 0.0252\text{ mol}$$

calculator.description: Divide the moles of water by 5 based on the stoichiometric ratio.

steps.title: Calculate empirical molar mass
steps.content:
Now, we divide the initial mass of the sample by the moles of the hydrate to find its molar mass ($M = \frac{m}{n}$).

$$\begin{aligned} M &= \frac{6.25\text{ g}}{0.0252\text{ mol}} \\ M &\approx 248.1\text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide initial mass by the calculated moles of hydrate.

ti84.instructions: Divide initial mass by the calculated moles of hydrate.

desmos.instructions: Divide initial mass by the calculated moles of hydrate.

calculator.description: Final molar mass calculation using the initial mass and moles.

steps.title: Verify with periodic table
steps.content:
In IB exams, if a question simply asks for the 'molar mass' of a known formula, you should sum the relative atomic masses from the periodic table. This matches the provided markscheme result.

$$\begin{aligned} M &= 63.5 + 32.1 + (4 \times 16.0) + 5 \times (2 \times 1.0 + 16.0) \\ M &= 249.6\text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Our experimental calculation ($248.1$) is very close to this theoretical value ($249.6$).

parts.content: Determine the mass of water lost during heating.
parts.markscheme:
Determine the mass lost:
$6.25\text{ g} - 3.98\text{ g} = 2.27\text{ g}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify given masses
steps.content:
To find the amount of water lost, we first need to identify the mass of the sample before and after heating from the question. 

*   **Initial mass** (hydrated copper(II) sulfate): $6.25\text{ g}$
*   **Final mass** (anhydrous copper(II) sulfate): $3.98\text{ g}$

highlights.text: initial mass of hydrated copper(II) sulfate was 6.25 g
mass of the remaining anhydrous solid was 3.98 g

steps.title: Apply Mass Difference Method
steps.content:
Based on the law of conservation of mass, the total mass of the products must equal the total mass of the reactants. When the hydrated crystals are heated, the water is released as a gas. Therefore, the **mass of water lost** is the difference between the initial hydrated mass and the final anhydrous mass:

$$\text{Mass of water lost} = \text{Initial mass} - \text{Final mass}$$

$$\text{Mass of water lost} = 6.25\text{ g} - 3.98\text{ g}$$

steps.title: Calculate and verify
steps.content:
By performing the subtraction, we find the total mass of water that evaporated during the reaction.

$$6.25\text{ g} - 3.98\text{ g} = 2.27\text{ g}$$

This calculation is a fundamental part of stoichiometry covered in **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**.

ti84.instructions: Enter the initial mass, press the minus key, then enter the final mass.

desmos.instructions: Type the subtraction directly into a new expression line.

parts.content: Calculate the number of moles of water lost.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate the moles of water:
$n(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) = \frac{2.27}{18.0} = 0.126\text{ mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Calculate mass of water lost
steps.content:
To find the amount of water lost, we first calculate the difference in mass between the hydrated salt (before heating) and the anhydrous salt (after heating).

$$\text{Mass of water lost} = \text{Initial mass} - \text{Final mass}$$

$$\text{Mass of water lost} = 6.25\text{ g} - 3.98\text{ g} = 2.27\text{ g}$$

calculator.description: Subtract the final mass from the initial mass.

highlights.text: initial mass of hydrated copper(II) sulfate was 6.25 g
mass of the remaining anhydrous solid was 3.98 g

steps.title: Apply the mole formula
steps.content:
We can now convert this mass into moles using the molar mass of water ($M = 18.01 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$, or $18.0 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$ as seen in the markscheme). We use the mole formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Where:
- $n$ is the number of moles
- $m$ is the mass in grams ($2.27\text{ g}$)
- $M$ is the molar mass ($18.0\text{ g mol}^{-1}$)

steps.title: Calculate final moles
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the formula to find the moles of water lost:

$$n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = \frac{2.27\text{ g}}{18.0\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.126\text{ mol}$$

This calculation aligns with **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass**, where you learned that the mole provides a bridge between the mass of a substance and the number of particles.

calculator.description: Divide the mass of water by its molar mass.

steps.title: Determine the anhydrous molar mass
steps.content:
First, we need to calculate the molar mass ($M$) of the remaining anhydrous copper(II) sulfate ($\text{CuSO}_4$). We sum the relative atomic masses ($A_r$) for each atom in the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} M(\text{CuSO}_4) &= 63.55 + 32.07 + 4(16.00) \\ &= 159.62 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This is a prerequisite for finding the amount in moles, which we need for stoichiometry.

steps.title: Calculate moles of anhydrous salt
steps.content:
Using the mass of the anhydrous solid remaining in the crucible, we calculate the number of moles ($n$) using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{CuSO}_4) &= \frac{3.98 \text{ g}}{159.62 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ &\approx 0.02493 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of anhydrous copper(II) sulfate.

steps.title: Apply the stoichiometric ratio
steps.content:
The balanced chemical equation shows that $1$ mole of $\text{CuSO}_4$ is produced alongside $5$ moles of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. 

$$\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{s}) \to \text{CuSO}_4(\text{s}) + 5\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{g})$$

To find the moles of water lost, we multiply the moles of anhydrous salt by the $1:5$ ratio:

$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) &= 5 \times n(\text{CuSO}_4) \\ &= 5 \times 0.02493 \dots \\ &\approx 0.125 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $0.125 \text{ mol}$. Note that the markscheme uses the mass difference method ($6.25 - 3.98 = 2.27 \text{ g}$), which gives $0.126 \text{ mol}$ due to slight experimental variations, but both approaches are chemically valid.

gdc.instructions: Multiply the previous answer by 5: Ans * 5 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous answer by 5: Ans * 5 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of water lost using the stoichiometric ratio.

steps.title: Identify the stoichiometric method
steps.content:
In this approach, we calculate the number of moles of water based on the chemical formula of the starting material, $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$. 

This method determines how many moles of water are theoretically present within a specific mass of the blue crystals. According to the formula, for every $1$ mole of hydrated copper(II) sulfate, there are exactly $5$ moles of water molecules trapped in the crystal lattice.

steps.title: Calculate hydrate molar mass
steps.content:
First, we need the molar mass ($M$) of the starting hydrate, $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$. Using values from the IB Data Booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} M(\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) &= 63.55 + 32.07 + (4 \times 16.00) + 5(2 \times 1.01 + 16.00) \\ &= 159.62 + 5(18.02) \\ &= 249.72 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find initial hydrate moles
steps.content:
We now calculate the moles ($n$) of the initial $6.25 \text{ g}$ sample of hydrated copper(II) sulfate using the formula from the Data Booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{6.25 \text{ g}}{249.72 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.02503 \text{ mol}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of the hydrate by dividing mass by molar mass.

highlights.text: initial mass of hydrated copper(II) sulfate was 6.25 g

steps.title: Apply stoichiometric ratio
steps.content:
From the balanced equation and the formula $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$, the molar ratio of hydrate to water is $1:5$. This means the moles of water released will be $5$ times the moles of the initial hydrate:

$$n(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) = 5 \times 0.02503 \text{ mol} = 0.12515 \text{ mol}$$

gdc.instructions: Press * 5 and EXE (this uses the 'Ans' feature).

ti84.instructions: Press * 5 and ENTER (this uses the 'Ans' feature).

calculator.description: Multiply the previous result by 5 to find moles of water.

steps.title: Compare with experimental result
steps.content:
Using the theoretical stoichiometry of the starting material, we calculate **$0.125 \text{ mol}$** of water.

However, in IB practical questions, marks are usually awarded for using the **experimental mass loss** ($6.25 \text{ g} - 3.98 \text{ g} = 2.27 \text{ g}$). Calculating moles from this measured mass loss gives:

$$n(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) = \frac{2.27 \text{ g}}{18.02 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.126 \text{ mol}$$

This slight difference exists because the experiment may not have reached $100\%$ completion or the crystals were not perfectly stoichiometric. For the exam mark, remember to prioritize the experimental data provided!

parts.content: Calculate the number of moles of $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ originally present.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate the moles of hydrate:
$n(\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) = \frac{6.25}{249.6} = 0.0250\text{ mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the given mass
steps.content: To find the number of moles originally present, we first identify the initial mass of the hydrated copper(II) sulfate provided in the question.

highlights.text: initial mass of hydrated copper(II) sulfate was 6.25 g

steps.title: Calculate the molar mass
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the molar mass ($M$) of $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ by summing the relative atomic masses from the periodic table:

$$\begin{aligned} M &= \text{Cu} + \text{S} + (4 \times \text{O}) + 5 \times (2 \times \text{H} + \text{O}) \\ M &= 63.55 + 32.06 + (4 \times 16.00) + 5 \times (18.02) \\ M &\approx 249.6 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This value represents the mass of one mole of the substance.

steps.title: Apply the mole formula
steps.content:
Using the relationship between mass, molar mass, and amount in moles from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Substitute our values into the equation:

$$n = \frac{6.25\text{ g}}{249.6\text{ g mol}^{-1}}$$

steps.title: Calculate using a GDC
steps.content: Use your graphing calculator to perform the division. This ensures accuracy and allows you to handle the precision of the numbers correctly.

calculator.description: Perform the division of mass by molar mass to find moles.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives $0.02504...$. Since the input mass $6.25$ has three significant figures, we round our final answer to three significant figures.

$$n(\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) = 0.0250\text{ mol}$$

This calculation earned 1 mark for the correct substitution and final value.

steps.title: Identify the mole ratio
steps.content:
To find the original number of moles of the hydrate, we first look at the balanced chemical equation. It shows that for every 1 mole of hydrated copper(II) sulfate $(\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O})$ that decomposes, exactly 1 mole of anhydrous copper(II) sulfate $(\text{CuSO}_4)$ is produced.

$$\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{s}) \to \text{CuSO}_4(\text{s}) + 5\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{g})$$

Since the ratio is $1:1$, the number of moles of the original hydrate is equal to the number of moles of the anhydrous residue left in the crucible.

steps.title: Calculate residue molar mass
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the molar mass ($M$) of the anhydrous residue, $\text{CuSO}_4$. Using the relative atomic masses from the periodic table:

$$\begin{aligned} M(\text{CuSO}_4) &= 63.55 + 32.07 + 4(16.00) \\ &= 159.62 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This value tells us how much one mole of the dry salt weighs.

steps.title: Calculate moles of CuSO4
steps.content:
Now, we use the mass of the remaining anhydrous solid ($3.98\text{ g}$) and the formula for moles from the data booklet to find the amount of $\text{CuSO}_4$ produced.

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Substituting our values:

$$n(\text{CuSO}_4) = \frac{3.98\text{ g}}{159.62\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0249 \text{ mol}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.98 divided by 159.62 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles using the mass of the anhydrous residue.

highlights.text: mass of the remaining anhydrous solid was 3.98 g

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Because of the $1:1$ stoichiometric ratio established in Step 0, the number of moles of $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ originally present is equal to the moles of anhydrous $\text{CuSO}_4$ calculated.

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the precision of the experimental data), we get:

$$n(\text{hydrate}) = 0.0249 \text{ mol}$$

*Note: The markscheme result of $0.0250 \text{ mol}$ is obtained using the initial mass of the hydrate ($6.25\text{ g}$), but calculating via the anhydrous residue is a valid alternative approach that yields a very similar result.*

methods.label: Calculation via Mass of Water Lost

steps.title: Analyze the reaction stoichiometry
steps.content:
To find the number of moles of the original hydrate using the water loss method, we first look at the balanced chemical equation. It shows that 1 mole of hydrated copper(II) sulfate produces 5 moles of water vapor upon heating.

$$\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s}) \to \mathrm{CuSO}_4(\mathrm{s}) + 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$$

This gives us a molar ratio of $1:5$ between the hydrate and the water lost.

steps.title: Calculate mass of water
steps.content:
The mass of water lost during the reaction is the difference between the initial mass of the hydrated sample and the final mass of the anhydrous solid.

$$\begin{aligned} m_{\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}} &= m_{\text{initial}} - m_{\text{final}} \\ m_{\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}} &= 6.25\text{ g} - 3.98\text{ g} \\ m_{\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}} &= 2.27\text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

This $2.27\text{ g}$ represents the total mass of the 5 water molecules that were released from the crystal structure.

calculator.description: Subtraction to find the mass of water lost.

highlights.text: initial mass of hydrated copper(II) sulfate was 6.25 g
mass of the remaining anhydrous solid was 3.98 g

steps.title: Calculate moles of water
steps.content:
Next, we convert the mass of water lost into moles using the formula for amount ($n$) from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

The molar mass ($M$) of water ($\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) is approximately $18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$.

$$\begin{aligned} n_{\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}} &= \frac{2.27\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ n_{\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}} &\approx 0.126\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Division to find moles of water.

steps.title: Relate to the hydrate
steps.content:
Using the $1:5$ molar ratio, we can find the original moles of the hydrate. Since 1 mole of hydrate contains 5 moles of water, the moles of hydrate is one-fifth of the moles of water lost:

$$\begin{aligned} n_{\text{hydrate}} &= \frac{n_{\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}}}{5} \\ n_{\text{hydrate}} &= \frac{0.12597...}{5} \\ n_{\text{hydrate}} &\approx 0.0252\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

This method uses the experimental decomposition data to determine the amount of substance present.

gdc.instructions: Divide the result of the previous step by 5.

ti84.instructions: Take the previous answer and divide by 5.

calculator.description: Divide moles of water by 5 to find moles of hydrate.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
While the water loss method gives $0.0252\text{ mol}$, the standard IB markscheme approach for finding the amount "originally present" is to use the starting mass and the molar mass of the hydrate directly:

$$n(\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) = \frac{6.25\text{ g}}{249.6\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0250\text{ mol}$$

The slight difference between the two results ($0.0252$ vs $0.0250$) is due to minor experimental measurement variations in the final anhydrous mass provided in the question.

parts.content: Using your answers, verify the empirical ratio of water to salt and comment on its consistency with the formula.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate the ratio:
$n(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) : n(\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) = 0.126 : 0.0250 = 5.04 : 1 \approx 5 : 1$ <marks>M1</marks>

Verify consistency:
The calculated ratio of $5:1$ confirms the stoichiometry of the formula $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Recall calculated mole amounts
steps.content:
To verify the ratio, we first need the amounts in moles of water lost and the original hydrated salt (or the anhydrous salt, as they are in a $1:1$ molar ratio). Based on the mass data and molar masses, we have:

- $n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = 0.126\text{ mol}$
- $n(\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}) = 0.0250\text{ mol}$

This calculation uses the fundamental formula for moles from the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

steps.title: Calculate the molar ratio
steps.content:
Now, we find the empirical ratio of water to the salt by dividing the number of moles of water by the number of moles of the hydrated salt:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{n(\text{H}_2\text{O})}{n(\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O})} = \frac{0.126}{0.0250}$$

Rounding to the nearest whole number gives a ratio of $5:1$.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of water moles to salt moles.

steps.title: Verify consistency with formula
steps.content:
The question asks us to comment on the consistency with the formula provided. 

The formula $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ indicates that for every $1$ mole of $\mathrm{CuSO}_4$, there are $5$ moles of $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ (water of crystallization). Since our calculated ratio is approximately $5:1$, the experimental results are **consistent** with the expected stoichiometry of the hydrate.

steps.title: Find experimental water percentage
steps.content:
First, we calculate the experimental percentage by mass of water. We know the mass of the water lost is the difference between the initial hydrated mass and the final anhydrous mass:

$$\text{Mass of } \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} = 6.25\text{ g} - 3.98\text{ g} = 2.27\text{ g}$$

Now, we calculate the experimental percentage of water in the sample:

$$\text{Experimental % } \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} = \frac{2.27\text{ g}}{6.25\text{ g}} \times 100 = 36.32\%$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 6.25 - 3.98, then divide the result by 6.25 and multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Divide the mass difference by the total initial mass and multiply by 100.

calculator.description: Calculate mass of water and experimental percentage.

highlights.text: initial mass of hydrated copper(II) sulfate was 6.25 g

steps.title: Calculate theoretical water percentage
steps.content:
To see if this is consistent with the formula $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$, we calculate the theoretical percentage by mass of water using molar masses ($M$):

$$\begin{aligned} M(\mathrm{CuSO}_4) &= 63.55 + 32.07 + (4 \times 16.00) = 159.62 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \\ M(5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) &= 5 \times (2 \times 1.01 + 16.00) = 90.10 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \\ M(\text{total}) &= 159.62 + 90.10 = 249.72 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

The theoretical percentage of water is:

$$\text{Theoretical % } \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} = \frac{90.10}{249.72} \times 100 = 36.08\%$$

desmos.instructions: Calculate the mass ratio of water to the total molar mass.

calculator.description: Calculate theoretical percentage of water in the hydrate.

steps.title: Verify ratio and consistency
steps.content:
Since the experimental percentage ($36.32\%$) is very close to the theoretical percentage ($36.08\%$), we can verify the molar ratio. 

We convert the experimental masses to moles using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\begin{aligned} n(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) &= \frac{2.27}{18.02} = 0.126 \text{ mol} \\ n(\mathrm{CuSO}_4) &= \frac{3.98}{159.62} = 0.0249 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

Ratio $= \frac{0.126}{0.0249} \approx 5.06 : 1$, which rounds to $5: 1$. This confirms the stoichiometry of $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$.
